mcqs.question: What is the correct way to declare an array of 100 structures of type struct Employee?
mcqs.explanation: In C, you declare an array by specifying the data type followed by array name and size. Since struct Employee is the type, option A is correct.

mcqs.question: Given: struct Book { char title[30]; int pages; }; struct Book library[5]; How do you access the pages member of the third book?
mcqs.explanation: To access a member of a structure in an array, use array[index].member notation. The third element is at index 2, so library[2].pages is correct.

mcqs.question: What will be the output if we initialize: struct Num { int x; }; struct Num arr[3] = {1, 2, 3};

options.text: All three elements will have x=1, x=2, x=3 respectively
All three elements will have x=1

mcqs.explanation: When initializing an array of structures with a list of values, each value initializes successive elements. arr[0].x=1, arr[1].x=2, arr[2].x=3.

mcqs.question: What is the total memory allocated for: struct Student { int id; char name[20]; float marks; }; struct Student class[25]; (Assume int=4, char=1, float=4 bytes, no padding)
mcqs.explanation: Structure size: int(4) + char[20](20) + float(4) = 28 bytes (may be padded to 32). For 25 elements: 25 × 32 = 800 bytes if padded, 700 if not.

mcqs.question: Which statement about array of structures is FALSE?

options.text: All structures in the array have the same memory layout
Elements are stored contiguously in memory
Array of structures can only contain primitive data types
We can use array indices to access different structures

mcqs.explanation: Structures can contain arrays, pointers, and other complex types. An array of structures can hold any type of data through the structure's members.

mcqs.question: What is the correct way to pass an array of structures to a function for modification?

options.text: Both A and B are correct

mcqs.explanation: Both syntaxes work: arr[] decays to pointer, and *arr is a pointer. Both pass the address, allowing modification of the original array.

mcqs.question: After this code: struct Test { int a; }; struct Test t[2] = {{10}, {20}}; printf("%d", t[1].a); What is the output?
mcqs.explanation: The initialization list {{10}, {20}} assigns 10 to t[0].a and 20 to t[1].a. Accessing t[1].a prints 20.

mcqs.question: What does this declaration mean? struct Employee *emp[50];

options.text: Pointer to an array of 50 Employees
Array of 50 pointers to Employee structures

mcqs.explanation: The [] has higher precedence than *, so emp[50] creates an array of 50 elements, each being a pointer (*) to struct Employee.

mcqs.question: Which approach is more memory efficient for large datasets?

options.text: Dynamically allocated array of structures using malloc
Neither is suitable for large data

mcqs.explanation: Dynamic allocation with malloc() doesn't use stack memory (limited), allowing much larger datasets. Local arrays are limited by stack size.

mcqs.question: What is the correct way to compare two entire structure elements in an array?
mcqs.explanation: C does not allow direct comparison of structures using ==. Use memcmp() or compare member-by-member. Option B uses memcmp for byte-level comparison.

mcqs.question: In a database of 1000 students, each with structure size 64 bytes, what is the address difference between student[0] and student[1]?
mcqs.explanation: Array elements are stored contiguously. The address difference equals the size of one structure element, which is 64 bytes.

mcqs.question: What is the output? struct Point { int x, y; }; struct Point p[3] = {1, 2, 3}; printf("%d", p[1].x);
mcqs.explanation: The initialization {1,2,3} only sets first three members sequentially: p[0].x=1, p[0].y=2, p[1].x=3. p[1].y and p[2] members are uninitialized, causing garbage values.

mcqs.question: Which is the most appropriate data structure for storing 500 student records with multiple fields?
mcqs.explanation: Array of structures organizes heterogeneous data efficiently, allowing easy access, sorting, and searching. Each element holds all related fields together.

mcqs.question: What happens if you access students[50] when the array is declared as students[50]?
mcqs.explanation: Array indices 0-49 are valid. Accessing index 50 is out of bounds and causes undefined behavior, possibly reading garbage or causing segmentation fault.

mcqs.question: How do you declare a constant array of structures?
mcqs.explanation: The const keyword should precede the type to make the array contents immutable. Both A and D work, but A is more common style.

mcqs.question: What is the purpose of using arrow operator (->) with array of structures?

options.text: When array element is accessed through a pointer

mcqs.explanation: The arrow operator (->) accesses structure members through a pointer: ptr->member is equivalent to (*ptr).member. Used when iterating with pointers.

mcqs.question: What is the correct initialization for an array of 3 structures with named initializers?
mcqs.explanation: C99 designated initializers use dot notation within braces. {{.id=1}, {.id=2}, {.id=3}} correctly initializes three separate elements.

mcqs.question: Which sorting algorithm complexity applies when sorting an array of n structures by comparing one integer member?
mcqs.explanation: Using bubble sort (or any comparison sort), the time complexity remains O(n²) regardless of what's being compared. The structure member size doesn't change algorithmic complexity.
